test_text.title: A Conversation between Sally and Peter about Their Day's Plans in a City

audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You'll hear two tourists, Sally and Peter, talking in a coffee shop.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You'll see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Peter, there you are.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: You've been ages.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: What kept you so long?
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: I'm sorry I'm so late, Sally.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Have you been waiting long?
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Oh, half an hour, but it doesn't matter.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: I've had a coffee, and I've been reading this guidebook for tourists.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Sally has been waiting half an hour for peter, so, c, has been circled.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Peter, there you are.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: You've been ages.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: What kept you so long?
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: I'm sorry I'm so late, Sally.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Have you been waiting long?
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Oh, half an hour, but it doesn't matter.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: I've had a coffee, and I've been reading this guidebook for tourists.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Sit down.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Hmm. You look very hot and tired.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Oh.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: What would you like to drink?
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: I'd love a really chilled mineral water or something.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Uh. Will you have another coffee?
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Yes, I will.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: The waitress will be back in a moment.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Why were you so late?
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Did something happen?
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Yes, you know, I went to the bank to cash some traveler's checks.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Well, the exchange rate was looking healthy, but when I went to the teller, they told me the computer system was temporarily down, so they couldn't do any transactions.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: They said the problem would be fixed in a few minutes, so I waited.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: And then I started talking to another guy in the bank, and I forgot the time.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Ha ha, really?
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Someone you met in the bank?
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Does he work there?
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: No, he was a tourist from New York.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: His name's Henry.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: And he's been here for a week, but he's moving on to Germany tomorrow.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: He's an architect, and he's spending four weeks traveling around Europe.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Just like us, yeah, just like us.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: He told me the names of some places where we should eat.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Great food and not too expensive, he said.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Oh, and he also gave me this map of the bus system.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: He said he didn't need it anymore.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: That's useful.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Pity he's moving on tomorrow.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Ah, here's the waitress.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Let's order.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Ah. Do you want anything to eat, or should we just have a drink?
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Well, I'm hungry, and we've got a lot of sightseeing to do, so let's just have a snack and a drink.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Sounds good to me.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Well, let's decide what we'll see today.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: I guess the best place to start is the Cathedral, and then the Castle.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Uh. What are the opening times for those two?
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Well, according to this guidebook, the Cathedral is only open from 9:30 in the morning until midday.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Oh, oh, no, hang on.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: That's the Cathedral museum.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Ah. The Cathedral itself is open morning and afternoon.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: The Castle is just open from 1 to 5, so we can't go there until after lunch.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Hmm. I really want to spend some time in the art gallery, because they've got this wonderful painting by Rembrandt that I've always wanted to see.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Um. What else should we see?
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Well, the guidebook says the Botanical Gardens are worth spending some time in.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: And they're open all day from 8 to 6, so we can go there anytime.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Hmm. I'd like to go to the markets near the river too, but oh, no, wait, that's only in the mornings too.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: As well as today and tomorrow, we can see some other places on Monday, you know.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: But I don't think the markets will be open then.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: They only open on Thursdays, so we've missed them for this week.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Maybe we should go to the Cathedral today, because it's Sunday tomorrow, and even though it's open every day, it might be more difficult to get in tomorrow because of the church services.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Hmm. That's true.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: But the art gallery isn't open on Sundays at all, so we'll have to go there today.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Hmm. The Castle is open every day except Mondays, so we're OK there.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: And the gardens of course only close at night.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Are all these places free, or do we have to pay to go in?
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Uh uh. What does the guidebook say?
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: I think there's a charge for all of them, except the botanical gardens.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Hmm. Oh, and the markets of course you don't pay to go in.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: OK, well it looks like our plan is this.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: We'll go to see the painting you like first, the Rembrandt, uh, then have lunch, and go on to the Castle after that, and then the Cathedral.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: OK, it says here that the roof of the Cathedral is really beautiful.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Is that right?
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: What I really want to do at the Cathedral is climb the tower.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: The view is supposed to be spectacular.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: OK, well, that'll be more than enough for today.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Then tomorrow, let's go to the botanical gardens and have a picnic.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Hmm, I want to sit by the river and watch the swans.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: This city is famous for them.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers. Now turn to section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items: Tourist attractions open all day: <input> and Gardens

The first place Peter and Sally will visit is the <input>.
At the Cathedral, Peter really wants to <input>.

answer_key.correct_answer: climb the tower | see the view

audioscript.text: Section 2, you'll hear part of a talk given by an international student counselor, speaking to a group of overseas students during their orientation week.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So the counseling services we offer deal with any problems arising from your studies or in your life outside the university.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Let's take academic counseling.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you're confused about subjects or how to combine them in your degree, then we can advise you and discuss the career you are aiming for.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So that you can see it all in context.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We can also chase up your tutor if you're not getting proper feedback on how you are getting on in your subject.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Besides help with academic problems, you may also need personal counseling.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you think you're already under stress, well, just wait till classes begin next week.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You'll have to start adjusting to teaching and learning methods.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That may be unfamiliar to you, as well as the mounting pressure as the deadline for that first assignment creeps up on you.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And of course you have to cope with all this without your usual social network.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You know the social contacts, family and friends, you could normally rely on for help.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: All of this causes anxiety.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Studying overseas can trigger a personal crisis.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You may have left a lot of what you might call unfinished business back in your own country, or you may have interrupted personal relationships, or even sometimes have broken them off to come overseas.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And so the student often feels lonely, unhappy, unmotivated, and unable to concentrate on studying.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Or there may be other things bothering you.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Our resident chaplain can offer you spiritual guidance if that's what you want, or we can put you in touch with community groups that can provide you with social contacts and friendship.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: What about exam stress?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It affects nearly everyone to some extent, but especially overseas students like yourselves.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There may be a huge amount of family pressure on you to succeed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And if you fail a subject or drop out of a course because it's too difficult, then your self-esteem can suffer.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But it's not the end of the world if you don't pass an exam.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I had to resit First Year Anthropology, so I can certainly offer you a sympathetic ear.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Anyway, exam failure can lead to worrying changes in the way you normally behave.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You may also be off your food, or you may have dietary problems because the local food is not to your liking and upsets you, and this can affect your health and studies.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Glenda Roberts is our dietitian in the health service, and we can put you onto her.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And we all have money problems, don't we?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But remember full-time students can get a low interest loan.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Of up to $600 to buy books, and for similar study related expenses.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And you can get double that amount if you can't afford an item of equipment you need for your course, uh, musical instrument for example.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And it doesn't stop there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When you move into a flat, starting up expenses including furniture for it can be covered by a loan through the welfare service.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: See Jill Freeman for details.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Can we help you?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, last academic year, in spite of staff cuts, we counseled 240 international students for a total of twenty-six hundred hours counseling.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And finally, we won all but just one of the 12 appeals that we launched on behalf of students.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Not too bad for an understaffed service, don't you think?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's all from me.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers. Now turn to section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Besides help with academic problems, you may also need personal counseling.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If you think you're already under stress, well, just wait till classes begin next week.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You'll have to start adjusting to teaching and learning methods.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That may be unfamiliar to you, as well as the mounting pressure as the deadline for that first assignment creeps up on you.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And of course you have to cope with all this without your usual social network.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You know the social contacts, family and friends, you could normally rely on for help.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: All of this causes anxiety.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Studying overseas can trigger a personal crisis.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You may have left a lot of what you might call unfinished business back in your own country, or you may have interrupted personal relationships, or even sometimes have broken them off to come overseas.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And so the student often feels lonely, unhappy, unmotivated, and unable to concentrate on studying.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Or there may be other things bothering you.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Our resident chaplain can offer you spiritual guidance if that's what you want, or we can put you in touch with community groups that can provide you with social contacts and friendship.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: What about exam stress?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It affects nearly everyone to some extent, but especially overseas students like yourselves.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There may be a huge amount of family pressure on you to succeed.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And if you fail a subject or drop out of a course because it's too difficult, then your self-esteem can suffer.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But it's not the end of the world if you don't pass an exam.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I had to resit First Year Anthropology, so I can certainly offer you a sympathetic ear.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Anyway, exam failure can lead to worrying changes in the way you normally behave.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You may also be off your food, or you may have dietary problems because the local food is not to your liking and upsets you, and this can affect your health and studies.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Glenda Roberts is our dietitian in the health service, and we can put you onto her.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And we all have money problems, don't we?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But remember full-time students can get a low interest loan.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Of up to $600 to buy books, and for similar study related expenses.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That's right.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And you can get double that amount if you can't afford an item of equipment you need for your course, uh, musical instrument for example.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And it doesn't stop there.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: When you move into a flat, starting up expenses including furniture for it can be covered by a loan through the welfare service.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: See Jill Freeman for details.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Can we help you?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, last academic year, in spite of staff cuts, we counseled 240 international students for a total of twenty-six hundred hours counseling.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And finally, we won all but just one of the 12 appeals that we launched on behalf of students.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Not too bad for an understaffed service, don't you think?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That's all from me.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Thank you.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers. Now turn to section 3.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: The Counselling Service may contact tutors if
options: they are too slow in marking assignments.
they give students a lot of work.
they don't inform students about their progress.

items.title: Stress may be caused by
International students may find stress difficult to handle because
options: they lack support from family and friends.
they don't have time to make new friends.
they find it difficult to socialize.

items.title: A personal crisis may be caused by
options: business problems in the student's own country.

items.title: Students may lose self-esteem if
options: their family puts too much pressure on them.

items.title: Students should consult Glenda Roberts if
options: they can't eat the local food.

items.title: Students in financial difficulties can receive
options: a loan to pay their course fees.
a no-interest loan to cover study expenses.

items.title: Loans are also available to students who
The number of students counselled by the service last year was
The speaker thinks the Counselling Service
options: has been effective in spite of staff shortages.
has suffered badly because of staff cuts.

test_text.title: Discussion on Assignment for Social Science Data Collection Methods

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You'll hear three friends discussing an assignment they're going to work on together.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Oh, there you are, good.
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: Oh, sorry, I'm a bit late.
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: There was a long queue.
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: So, have you worked out how to deal with this assignment then?
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: Not yet.
audioscript.speaker: MICK

audioscript.text: We've only been here a couple of minutes ourselves.
audioscript.speaker: MICK

audioscript.text: Can you just remind me what the task is exactly?
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: Well, there are two, uh, no, three parts to it.
audioscript.speaker: PETE

audioscript.text: First we've got to write an essay about ways of collecting data.
audioscript.speaker: PETE

audioscript.text: Then, what's the title of the essay exactly?
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: I've got it here.
audioscript.speaker: MICK

audioscript.text: Uh. Assess the two main methods of collecting data in social science research.
audioscript.speaker: MICK

audioscript.text: And how much do we need to write?
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: Fifteen hundred words.
audioscript.speaker: MICK

audioscript.text: That's for the essay.
audioscript.speaker: MICK

audioscript.text: Then for the second part of the assignment, we have to choose one method of data collection, and carry out a small scale study, making appropriate use of the method chosen to gather data from at least five subjects.
audioscript.speaker: MICK

audioscript.text: And then we have to write a report on the study.
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: That's right, of three to four thousand words.
audioscript.speaker: PETE

audioscript.text: Did you get as far as discussing which form of data collection we should go for?
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: Questionnaire or interview, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: Yeah, I think we should use a questionnaire.
audioscript.speaker: MICK

audioscript.text: It'll be so much less time consuming than organizing interviews, I reckon.
audioscript.speaker: MICK

audioscript.text: Once we've agreed on the wording of it, we only have to send it out and wait for the responses.
audioscript.speaker: MICK

audioscript.text: Yes, I think it probably would be quicker.
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: But what did that article he gave us last week say about the quality of data from questionnaires?
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: I'm pretty sure it recommended questionnaires as a source of highly reliable data.
audioscript.speaker: MICK

audioscript.text: As long as you design the questionnaire properly in the first place, the data will be fine.
audioscript.speaker: MICK

audioscript.text: No, I'm sure it talked about drawbacks as well, didn't it?
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: Something about the response rate and the problems you get if it's too low.
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: Yep, but we only need data from 5 subjects anyway.
audioscript.speaker: MICK

audioscript.text: I suppose so.
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: Another drawback I remember it mentioned was that questionnaire data tends not to reveal anything unexpected, because it is limited to the questions fixed in advance by the researcher.
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: Come on, Rosa.
audioscript.speaker: MICK

audioscript.text: This is only a practice.
audioscript.speaker: MICK

audioscript.text: It's not meant to be real research, is it?
audioscript.speaker: MICK

audioscript.text: Well, I'm not sure about that.
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Maybe I'd better go through the article again, just to be sure.
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: Can you remember what it was called?
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: Sample surveys in social science research, I think.
audioscript.speaker: MICK

audioscript.text: By Mehta.
audioscript.speaker: MICK

audioscript.text: MEHTA.
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: MICK

audioscript.text: And he also recommended a more recent book called Survey Research, by Bell, I think.
audioscript.speaker: MICK

audioscript.text: It's in that series published by London University.
audioscript.speaker: MICK

audioscript.text: And if we try to use interviews instead, I saw a book in the departmental library that'll be helpful.
audioscript.speaker: PETE

audioscript.text: It's called Interviews That Work by Wilson, published in Oxford in 1988.
audioscript.speaker: PETE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: Oh, I've got a tutorial now.
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: Can we meet up again later this week?
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: What about Friday morning?
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: Suits me. 11 o'clock?
audioscript.speaker: PETE

audioscript.text: Fine.
audioscript.speaker: ROSA

audioscript.text: Before Friday, I think we should all look through the reading list.
audioscript.speaker: MICK

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers. Now turn to section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

desc.constraint: NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS AND/OR A NUMBER

items: Title: 'Assess the two main methods of <input> in social science research'
items.title: Part 1: Essay

items: Gather data from at least <input> subjects.
items.title: Part 2: Small-scale study
Part 3: Report on study

body.title: What TWO disadvantages of the questionnaire form of data collection do the students discuss?
options: It is less likely to reveal the unexpected.
It can only be used with literate populations.
There is a delay between the distribution and return of questionnaires.

desc.constraint: NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS OR A NUMBER

answer_key.correct_answer: collecting data | gathering data | data collection

test_text.title: Corporate Crime: Ignored, Costly, and Consequential

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You'll hear the beginning of one lecture in a series of lectures about crime.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So far in these lectures, we've been looking at crimes like robbery and murder, both from a historical viewpoint, and also in contemporary society.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And we've seen that the preoccupation in western society with crime and with lawlessness is part of a long and continuous tradition, rather than something which is new and unique to modern society.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But over the past 70 years or so, there has been a massive increase in one type of crime, which is what's known as corporate crime.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Corporate crime is crime which as the name suggests is connected with companies, with business organizations.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It includes illegal acts of either individuals or a group within the company.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But what is important is that these acts are normally in accordance with the goals of the company, they're for the good of the company, rather than the individual.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's been defined as quote crime which is committed for the corporate organization, the company, not against it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Unquote.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So crimes like theft by employees, things like embezzlement or fraud against one's actual employer are excluded, according to this definition.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The employees may be involved, but they're acting in the first place for the company.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They may not even realize they're committing a crime, or they may realize but they feel it's excusable because it's policy, or because otherwise they may lose their jobs.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So here really we're talking about the links between power and crime.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now this is one area that much less is generally known about than conventional or traditional crime.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It has been relatively ignored by the mass media.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For example, it tends to be under reported in comparison with conventional crime in news broadcasts, and in crime serials and films and so on.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They very rarely deal with corporate crime, and it also tends to be ignored in academic circles.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's been far more research on conventional crime, and far more data is available.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are several reasons for this lack of interest in corporate crime compared with other types of crime.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's often very complex, whereas with conventional crime, it's usually possible to follow what's going on without specialist knowledge.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As well as this, whereas conventional crime usually has a lot of human interest, corporate crime often has much less.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The third reason, and possibly the most significant one, is that very often the victims are unaware.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They think their misfortune is an accident, or that it's the fault of no one in particular.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They're unaware that they've been victims of a crime.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So when we look at the effects of corporate crime, we may find it's very difficult to assess the costs, but these costs can be very considerable in both their economic and social aspects.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Let's look at the economic cost first.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For example, if a company is producing fruit juice, and it dilutes its product so that it's just a little below the concentration it should be.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Many millions of people may be paying a small amount extra for their carton of orange juice.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now small amounts like this may seem insignificant for individual customers, too small to worry about.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But for the company, this deception might result in massive illegal profit.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, all studies of corporate crime agree that the individuals are in fact deprived of far more money by such crime than they are by conventional crime like robbery and theft.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In addition to this, we have to consider the social costs of corporate crime, and these are again very difficult to assess, but they are considerable.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They're important.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Because they can undermine the faith of the public in the business world, and also more importantly, because the main group of people they affect are in fact not the richer sections of society.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But the poorer.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So here companies are robbing the poor to benefit the rich.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are two more points to do with corporate crime that I'd like to illustrate with reference to a specific event, which occurred several years ago.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This was an explosion of a large oil tanker.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Which caused the loss of more than 50 lives of the crew.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It was an explosion which never should have happened, and a subsequent inquiry laid the blame not on anyone who had actually been on the tanker at the time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But on the owners of the tanker, they had deliberately decided not to carry out necessary repair work on the tanker, as it was due to be sold.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And it was this lack of repair work, which was directly responsible for the explosion.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now this illustrates two points to do with corporate crime.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: First of all, that it does not have to be intentional.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The owners of the tanker certainly did not intend it to explode.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But very serious consequences can result from people or organizations not considering the possible results of their actions seriously enough.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The main crime here was indifference to the human results, rather than actual intention to harm anyone, but that didn't make the results any less tragic.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And this leads me to my second point, that corporate crime can have very severe consequences.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's not just a matter of companies making bigger profits than they should do.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But of events which may affect the lives of innocent people, and yet very often companies because they say they didn't intend to harm anyone, can avoid taking responsibility for the results of their actions, and that has been a very dangerous loophole in the law.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A further example of corporate crime was...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That that is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: That is the end of the listening test.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: In the IELTS test, you would now have 10 minutes to transfer your answers to the listening answer sheet.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You'll hear the beginning of one lecture in a series of lectures about crime.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: So far in these lectures, we've been looking at crimes like robbery and murder, both from a historical viewpoint, and also in contemporary society.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And we've seen that the preoccupation in western society with crime and with lawlessness is part of a long and continuous tradition, rather than something which is new and unique to modern society.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But over the past 70 years or so, there has been a massive increase in one type of crime, which is what's known as corporate crime.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Corporate crime is crime which as the name suggests is connected with companies, with business organizations.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It includes illegal acts of either individuals or a group within the company.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But what is important is that these acts are normally in accordance with the goals of the company, they're for the good of the company, rather than the individual.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's been defined as quote crime which is committed for the corporate organization, the company, not against it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Unquote.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So crimes like theft by employees, things like embezzlement or fraud against one's actual employer are excluded, according to this definition.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The employees may be involved, but they're acting in the first place for the company.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They may not even realize they're committing a crime, or they may realize but they feel it's excusable because it's policy, or because otherwise they may lose their jobs.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So here really we're talking about the links between power and crime.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now this is one area that much less is generally known about than conventional or traditional crime.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It has been relatively ignored by the mass media.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For example, it tends to be under reported in comparison with conventional crime in news broadcasts, and in crime serials and films and so on.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They very rarely deal with corporate crime, and it also tends to be ignored in academic circles.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There's been far more research on conventional crime, and far more data is available.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There are several reasons for this lack of interest in corporate crime compared with other types of crime.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's often very complex, whereas with conventional crime, it's usually possible to follow what's going on without specialist knowledge.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As well as this, whereas conventional crime usually has a lot of human interest, corporate crime often has much less.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The third reason, and possibly the most significant one, is that very often the victims are unaware.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They think their misfortune is an accident, or that it's the fault of no one in particular.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They're unaware that they've been victims of a crime.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So when we look at the effects of corporate crime, we may find it's very difficult to assess the costs, but these costs can be very considerable in both their economic and social aspects.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Let's look at the economic cost first.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For example, if a company is producing fruit juice, and it dilutes its product so that it's just a little below the concentration it should be.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Many millions of people may be paying a small amount extra for their carton of orange juice.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now small amounts like this may seem insignificant for individual customers, too small to worry about.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But for the company, this deception might result in massive illegal profit.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, all studies of corporate crime agree that the individuals are in fact deprived of far more money by such crime than they are by conventional crime like robbery and theft.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In addition to this, we have to consider the social costs of corporate crime, and these are again very difficult to assess, but they are considerable.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They're important.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Because they can undermine the faith of the public in the business world, and also more importantly, because the main group of people they affect are in fact not the richer sections of society.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But the poorer.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So here companies are robbing the poor to benefit the rich.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There are two more points to do with corporate crime that I'd like to illustrate with reference to a specific event, which occurred several years ago.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This was an explosion of a large oil tanker.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Which caused the loss of more than 50 lives of the crew.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It was an explosion which never should have happened, and a subsequent inquiry laid the blame not on anyone who had actually been on the tanker at the time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But on the owners of the tanker, they had deliberately decided not to carry out necessary repair work on the tanker, as it was due to be sold.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And it was this lack of repair work, which was directly responsible for the explosion.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now this illustrates two points to do with corporate crime.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: First of all, that it does not have to be intentional.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The owners of the tanker certainly did not intend it to explode.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But very serious consequences can result from people or organizations not considering the possible results of their actions seriously enough.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The main crime here was indifference to the human results, rather than actual intention to harm anyone, but that didn't make the results any less tragic.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And this leads me to my second point, that corporate crime can have very severe consequences.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's not just a matter of companies making bigger profits than they should do.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But of events which may affect the lives of innocent people, and yet very often companies because they say they didn't intend to harm anyone, can avoid taking responsibility for the results of their actions, and that has been a very dangerous loophole in the law.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A further example of corporate crime was...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That that is the end of Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: That is the end of the listening test.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: In the IELTS test, you would now have 10 minutes to transfer your answers to the listening answer sheet.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

Corporate crime does NOT include
Corporate crime has been ignored by:

items: a) often more complex and needing <input>
b) less human interest than conventional crime
items.title: Reasons:

items: cause more losses to individuals than conventional crimes
make people lose trust in business world

items.title: Effects:

body.title: The oil tanker explosion was an example of a crime which
options: made a large profit for the company.
